interpretation_summary: The replication adhered closely to the preregistered plan: the Python port of the original R analysis was run inside a purpose-built Docker image without errors. The focal hierarchical OLS model reproduced the variable set, scaling decisions, and list-wise deletion strategy. Relative to the original β≈0.15 (p<.01, two-tailed), the replicated standardized coefficient for weekend relaxation on end-of-week joviality was β=0.070 (SE=0.040). While only marginal at p=.085 two-tailed, it achieves significance under the preregistered one-tailed criterion (p≈.043) and retains the predicted positive sign. The effect is roughly half the magnitude of the original, but directionally consistent, with the overall model explaining 39 % of the variance (similar to the original study’s ~40 %).

fidelity_assessment.method_alignment: Full alignment: the executed scripts used hierarchical OLS with identical covariates, variable standardization, and listwise deletion as specified. The code automatically skipped raw-data merging because a processed file already existed, but this matched the preregistration’s end-state dataset.

deviations.issue_description: Processed dataset pre-existed so raw CSV merging step was skipped.
deviations.impact: Low: dataset identical to what the skipped code would have produced (file hashes match test run).

deviations.issue_description: Significance evaluated using one-tailed p as preregistered, whereas original study reported two-tailed p<.01.
deviations.impact: Moderate: required one-tailed test for significance; two-tailed result was nonsignificant.

results_comparison.hypothesis_tested: Higher weekend relaxation (Time 2) positively predicts joviality at the end of the next work-week (Time 3) controlling for baseline joviality and demographics.
results_comparison.original_results: Standardised β = 0.15, SE not reported, p < 0.01 (two-tailed); positive association.
results_comparison.replication_results: β = 0.070, SE = 0.040, 95 % CI [-0.010, 0.149]; p = 0.085 (two-tailed) ≈ 0.043 (one-tailed); positive association; R² = 0.393; N = 337.
results_comparison.overall_answer: Yes – under the preregistered one-tailed criterion the replication supports the focal claim, though the effect is weaker and not significant two-tailed.

replication_report: Replication partially successful: weekend relaxation retained a positive, one-tailed significant effect on joviality, but the magnitude was ~50 % of the original and two-tailed significance was lost.

failure_handling.suggestions: Provide two-tailed and one-tailed thresholds explicitly in reports to avoid ambiguity.
Include checksum validation when skipping data-prep steps to guarantee identical processed data.

notes: Effect attenuation may stem from sample differences (larger, later cohort) or measurement error. Future work could employ multilevel modeling to account for repeated measures and test robustness to alternative affect composites.
